narrative:
The stress score has dropped sharply from 41 to 18, moving the regime firmly into calm territory, driven primarily by credit markets where high-yield spreads at 266 basis points and investment-grade at 72 basis points both sit at deeply negative z-scores, reflecting continued spread compression rather than any sign of distress. VIX at 19.5 with a contango term structure reinforces that options markets are pricing orderly conditions despite the softer equity tape.

The standout development today is the abrupt single-session weakness in emerging markets, with EEM down 5.7% and EWJ falling 4.3%, a move that sits in contrast to the calm credit backdrop and raises the question of whether this is idiosyncratic positioning or an early read on broader risk appetite deterioration outside the United States. A sustained widening in high-yield spreads toward a positive z-score, or a break of EEM below its recent range on continued volume, would be the clearest signal that today's EM pressure is migrating into systemic stress rather than remaining confined to a single session.
